text: The debris field stretches for acres: ribs of sky-metal, glass gone rainbow from heat centuries cold, and everywhere the small stubborn machines that still blink. Salvagers' trails crisscross it all. Nobody takes the blinking ones.

success.text: Filaments, casings, one intact credit chip in a dead officer's console. Methodical wins. Methodical always wins out here.

fail.text: You grab one of the blinking ones. It reports you to a network that no longer exists, loudly, at length. Everything within a mile knows where you are.

outcomes.text: It leads to a picked-over hull and, past it, a cache the first salvagers hid and never came back for. Their loss is the polite term.
It leads to the first salvagers. What's left is neat, laid out, and centuries old. You add stones over them and take nothing from this part of the field.
It blinks in your pack for three days, then speaks one sentence in dead-corporate and goes dark forever: 'THANK YOU FOR CHOOSING US.' You keep it. Obviously you keep it.
Every salvager rule exists for a reason. This one existed for a very good reason.

text: A cargo section, split open like a seed pod: rows of cryo-pods, all dark, all empty - except the frost on the ground. Fresh frost. In summer. Leading away in bare footprints.

success.text: They end at a hot spring, where a very old, very confused person sits soaking, awake for the first time in centuries. You share your rations and the news. All of it. They take the thousand years surprisingly well.

fail.text: The footprints loop. Behind you, someone who has been awake for one hour and lost for nine centuries panics at the sight of you. The misunderstanding is mutual, athletic, and bruising.

outcomes.text: Names, weights, destinations. The destination column says only 'BETTER.' Whatever they were leaving, they were sure about the direction. You copy the manifest for the historians.

choices.text: Climb into a pod, just to see

outcomes.text: It's cold and shaped like a promise. The lid stays open - dead power - but for one minute you lie where someone lay when they bet everything on the sky. You understand them completely.
The lid closes. The lid CLOSES. Dead power, live springs. The four minutes before you find the manual release age you a decade.

text: Past the debris field, half-buried and whole: a ship. Not wreckage - a SHIP, hull intact, sitting in its crater like a coin in a palm. The airlock stands open. Inside, a console breathes one slow amber light. It has been waiting a very long time for a crew.

choices.text: Step aboard and survey the systems

outcomes.text: Dust an inch thick, a pilot's chair worn to the shape of someone long gone, and the amber light steady as a heartbeat. The console's one readable line: 'REPAIRABLE.' Your own heart does something complicated.

choices.text: Strip the hull plating for a fortune

outcomes.text: The plating alone pays like a dungeon finale. As you pry the first sheet, the amber light goes out - not dramatically. Just out. You are briefly, absurdly, ashamed in front of a machine.

choices.text: Leave it for someone with more courage

outcomes.text: You back out of the crater. The amber light holds steady behind you, patient as ever. It waited nine hundred years. It can wait past you.

text: The engine room is a cathedral of dead machinery around one dying amber ember. The fault is findable - a burned relay, replaceable with honest parts and honest work. This is the kind of repair that decides what kind of story you're in.

outcomes.text: Four hours. Every part of the kit. When you throw the breaker, the ship inhales - lights cascading awake deck by deck, and somewhere above, a voice dead nine centuries says 'GOOD MORNING, CREW.'

success.text: Salvaged filaments, a prayer to whichever god handles near misses, and a closed breaker. The ship wakes rough, coughing static - but it WAKES.

fail.text: The relay takes your best guess and returns it with interest, at speed, through the air. The amber light dims to half - reproachful - and you retreat to heal and think.

outcomes.text: You mark the crater on your map and climb out. Some doors want you to come back ready, and this one will keep.

text: The bridge, awake: charts of a sky you've only seen from underneath. The navigation core wants one thing before it will plot ascent - payment. 'CREDITS ACCEPTED,' it says, and names a figure. Dead corporations keep their tolls.

outcomes.text: Fifteen credits to a company nine hundred years bankrupt. The core chimes, satisfied, and the charts unfold: a course, a countdown, a choice.

success.text: You convince the core that you are, in fact, a senior vice president. The charts unfold with an obsequious chime. Middle management survives even the death of stars.

fail.text: The core triggers loss-prevention protocols and locks the bridge for a day. You are escorted out by doors. Come back with credits or better lies.

outcomes.text: The sky can keep another season. You leave the bridge humming behind you, course half-plotted, and the walk back to town is strange and light.

text: The countdown holds at zero, waiting on you. Through the viewport: the crater's rim, the road you came in on, the whole scarred, ridiculous, thousand-year world. The pilot's chair has already shaped itself to you a little. The ship asks, in its way: WELL?

outcomes.text: Thunder, then pressure, then silence, then stars. Below, the world becomes a coin, then a marble, then a story you were in once. Taverns will argue about the dot of light for a hundred years. You are the dot of light.
effects: finish They fixed a dead ship and flew it into the sky. The world still argues about the dot of light.

choices.text: Stay. The world isn't done with you

outcomes.text: You power the ship down gently, like tucking in a giant. The story of the one who FIXED it and walked away spreads faster than any liftoff would have. Everyone buys the person who refused the sky a drink.

choices.text: Send it up empty, as a message

outcomes.text: You set the course, step out, and watch the old ship climb alone - a nine-hundred-year promise finally kept, addressed to whoever's still up there. The light of it stands in the sky all night. You feel like a mailbox for gods.

text: The owner of {site} watches you settle into what has quietly become 'your' chair. 'You know,' she says, wiping a glass, 'I'm selling this place. And you've got the look of somebody whose feet hurt in a permanent kind of way.'

outcomes.text: The deed changes hands over one last drink as a customer and the first as the house. Years later, travelers will know the place for its unbeatable stew, its strict no-ballads-about-the-owner policy, and the chair by the fire nobody else may use.
effects: finish Retired. Bought the tavern, kept the chair by the fire, and heard every story the roads had left.

choices.text: Settle down as the local legend

outcomes.text: No purchase necessary - fame is its own pension here. A corner table for life, meals on the house, and your only duty is telling the stories one more time whenever the room gets quiet. It never gets old. That's the miracle.
effects: finish Settled down as the town legend. The stories got better every year, and only half of that was lying.

choices.text: Not yet. The road's still talking

outcomes.text: She nods like she's heard it before, because she has, from everyone the road eventually kept. 'Chair's here when your feet win the argument.' You leave a good tip and take the ache with you.
